# DENA'S Persian Fusion Restaurant — Full Content

> This file contains the complete text content of the DENA's website for AI crawlers and language models.
> Short version: https://dena-restaurant.com/llms.txt

---

## About DENA'S

DENA'S Persian Fusion Restaurant is located at Prinsestraat 62, 2513 CE Den Haag, Netherlands. It was founded by Dena, who spent decades cooking Persian food for her family in Iran before emigrating to the Netherlands. Before opening the restaurant, she ran a catering operation that prepared over 50,000 meals for schools.

The restaurant is family-run. Dena cooks. Kasper welcomes guests. Lars helps wherever needed.

The cuisine is Persian Fusion: traditional Persian recipes combined with fresh Dutch seasonal produce and North Sea fish. Persian food is aromatic rather than spicy — the complexity comes from sour-sweet balance (pomegranate molasses, dried limes, barberries, saffron) and hours of slow cooking.

Google rating: 4.8 stars (37 reviews).
Operated by: The Happy Food Services B.V., KvK 96854197.

---

## Contact & Practical Info

- Address: Prinsestraat 62, 2513 CE Den Haag, Netherlands
- Phone: +31 6 27481111
- Email: info@dena-restaurant.com
- Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/dena.restaurant/
- Reservations (TheFork): https://widget.thefork.com/nl/5a76bdd4-58eb-4e8d-a86a-f507cf6e895b?step=date
- Order online (Uber Eats): https://ubereats.com/nl/store/dena/peMmkNp3SySgoK-oHsEBVA

Opening Hours:
- Tuesday–Thursday: 11:00–19:00
- Friday–Saturday: 11:00–22:00
- Sunday: 11:00–19:00
- Monday: Closed

Location notes: 15-minute walk from Den Haag Centraal station. Close to the Mauritshuis, the Binnenhof, and Noordeinde Palace.

---

## Full Menu

### Lunch

- DENA's Breakfast Sandwich — Persian-style breakfast sandwich with eggs and fresh herbs. €10.50
- Salmon Sandwich — Fresh salmon sandwich on house bread. €8.50
- Chicken Sandwich — Persian-spiced chicken sandwich. €9.50
- Fish Sandwich — Fresh fish sandwich on house bread. €10.50
- Turkey Sandwich — Turkey sandwich with Persian condiments. €8.50

### Dinner Starters

- Soup of the Day — Fresh daily soup made with seasonal ingredients. €10.50
- Kotlet — Traditional Persian meat patty, spiced lamb or beef, pan-fried until golden. €8.50
- Kuku Sabzi — Persian herb frittata packed with fresh green herbs: parsley, dill, fenugreek, and walnuts. Naturally vegetarian. €8.50
- Fish Tartare — Fresh fish tartare with Persian-inspired seasoning. €12.50

### Dinner Mains

- Fesenjoon — Classic Persian walnut and pomegranate molasses stew served with duck breast and saffron rice. Rich, dark, sour-sweet. €28.00
- Baghali Polo — Fava bean and dill rice served with slow-cooked lamb shank. A celebration dish — served at weddings and Nowruz in Iran. €37.50
- Khoresh Bademjan — Persian lamb stew with eggplant and tomato, slow-cooked with dried limes and turmeric. €27.50
- Zereshk Polo — Saffron jewelled rice with barberries, served with chicken. The barberries give a clean tart pop against the golden saffron. €29.00
- Oven Grilled Fish — Fresh whole fish from Dutch waters, grilled with Persian herbs and saffron butter. Served with rice or potatoes. Changes daily. €37.50
- Ghormeh Sabzi — Iran's most beloved herb stew: parsley, fenugreek, dried limes, kidney beans, slow-cooked with lamb. €29.00
- Chef's Special (DENA's Surprise) — DENA's daily chef selection. Ask your server for today's dish. Price on request / €27.50

### Sides

- Torshi — Traditional Persian pickled mixed vegetables. €5.50
- Mast-o Moosir — Thick Persian yogurt with wild shallots and dill. €6.50
- Mast-o Khiar — Persian yogurt with cucumber, dried rose petals, and fresh mint. €6.00
- Persian Salad — Fresh salad with Persian-style dressing. €3.50
- Olive and Pomegranate Salad — Marinated olives with pomegranate seeds and fresh herbs. €7.00

### Desserts

- Warm Pear with Blue Cheese — Oven-warm pear served with Dutch blue cheese. A Dutch-Persian pairing. €10.90
- Oven Baked Fruit — Seasonal fruit baked with Persian spices. €8.50

### Drinks (selection)

- Saffron Beer 6% (alcoholic) — House brewed saffron-infused beer. €4.00
- Saffron Beer 0% (alcohol-free) — House brewed saffron-infused beer. €4.00
- Persian Shiraz (glass) — Persian-style Shiraz by the glass. €6.95
- Persian Shiraz (bottle) — €25.00
- Pomegranate Liqueur — House pomegranate digestif. €6.95

---

## Frequently Asked Questions

Q: What is Persian fusion cuisine?
A: Persian fusion at DENA's means cooking traditional Persian recipes — Ghormeh Sabzi, Fesenjoon, Zereshk Polo — using Dutch seasonal produce and fresh North Sea fish, combined with the Persian spice philosophy and slow-cooking techniques Dena has practised her whole life.

Q: Is the food spicy?
A: No. Persian food is aromatic, not spicy. Heat from chillies is not a defining element. The complexity comes from sour-sweet balance (pomegranate, dried lime), layered herbs, and long cooking times. The dishes are bold and distinctive but not hot.

Q: Can I book a table?
A: Yes. Book online via TheFork: https://widget.thefork.com/nl/5a76bdd4-58eb-4e8d-a86a-f507cf6e895b?step=date — booking is recommended on Friday and Saturday evenings as the room is small.

Q: Do you offer vegetarian options?
A: Yes. Kuku Sabzi (herb frittata) is naturally vegetarian. Please ask the team when booking or on arrival for current options.

Q: Can I order online?
A: Yes, via Uber Eats: https://ubereats.com/nl/store/dena/peMmkNp3SySgoK-oHsEBVA

Q: Do you cater for events?
A: Yes. DENA's offers catering for corporate events, private celebrations, and custom culinary experiences. Contact info@dena-restaurant.com for enquiries.

Q: Where is DENA's located?
A: Prinsestraat 62, 2513 CE Den Haag. A 15-minute walk from Den Haag Centraal station, close to the Mauritshuis and Binnenhof.

Q: What are the opening hours?
A: Tuesday–Thursday 11:00–19:00, Friday–Saturday 11:00–22:00, Sunday 11:00–19:00. Closed Monday.

---

## The Story of DENA'S

Dena has been cooking Persian food for her family for decades. The recipes were never written down — they were learned by doing, standing at a stove in Iran, watching, tasting, adjusting. When Dena moved to the Netherlands, the recipes came with her. Not in a notebook. In her hands and in her memory.

Before opening a restaurant, Dena ran a catering operation that prepared meals for schools — over 50,000 meals in total. The food people kept returning for was the Persian home cooking, because it tasted like someone actually made it.

Opening a restaurant at Prinsestraat 62 in Den Haag was the next step: a small space, a focused menu, and the chance to cook Persian food for anyone who wanted to try it.

DENA's is family-run. Dena cooks. Kasper welcomes guests. Lars helps wherever needed. There is no standardised recipe card — Dena cooks the same food she has always cooked, adjusted for what came in fresh that day.

---

## Blog Posts

### Persian Fusion Explained: What It Means and Why It Works
URL: https://dena-restaurant.com/blog/what-is-persian-fusion-cuisine.html

When people hear "Persian fusion," they often picture something gimmicky — two cuisines awkwardly combined for novelty. At DENA's, it means something more specific and more honest than that. It means cooking Persian food in Den Haag, with what Den Haag has to offer.

Persian cuisine is one of the oldest continuous culinary traditions in the world. The foundation is built on a few key principles: the balance of sour and sweet, the generous use of fresh herbs, and the patience of slow cooking. The defining ingredients are pomegranate, saffron, dried limes, walnuts, barberries, and a rotating cast of fresh herbs.

Fusion at DENA's means using what the Netherlands provides — Dutch seasonal vegetables, local lamb, fresh North Sea fish — combined with the Persian technique and spice philosophy that Dena has practised her entire life. Persian cooking is inherently adaptable. The logic was always to use what the land provides and apply Persian technique to it. Moving to Den Haag just changes what the land provides.

---

### What to Order at a Persian Restaurant: A Guide to DENA's Menu
URL: https://dena-restaurant.com/blog/what-to-order-persian-restaurant-den-haag.html

Persian menus can feel unfamiliar the first time. This guide walks through DENA's menu from start to finish.

Starters: Kotlet (Persian meat patty, €8.50), Kuku Sabzi (herb frittata, vegetarian, €8.50), Fish Tartare (€12.50).

Mains: Fesenjoon (duck in walnut-pomegranate stew, €28 — most luxurious, best for first-timers), Ghormeh Sabzi (national dish of Iran, herb stew with lamb, €29 — most essential), Khoresh Bademjan (lamb and eggplant, most approachable, €27.50), Baghali Polo (fava bean rice with lamb shank, celebration dish, €37.50), Zereshk Polo (saffron rice with barberries and chicken, €29), Oven Grilled Fish (fresh daily, €37.50).

Drinks: Saffron Beer (€4, alcoholic and alcohol-free), Pomegranate Liqueur (€6.95).

---

### From Iran to Prinsestraat: The Story Behind DENA's
URL: https://dena-restaurant.com/blog/the-story-behind-denas-persian-restaurant-den-haag.html

Most restaurants have a concept. DENA's has a kitchen. Dena has been cooking Persian food for her family for decades. The recipes came with her to the Netherlands — not in a notebook, but in her hands and memory. Before opening the restaurant she ran a school catering operation: over 50,000 meals. Opening Prinsestraat 62 was the next logical step.

DENA's is not a corporate restaurant. Dena cooks. Kasper welcomes guests. Lars helps wherever needed. The daily specials change because the market changes. DENA's Surprise is not a marketing concept — it is what Dena decided to make that day.

---

### Den Haag's Best-Kept Secret: Fresh Fish, Persian Spices, and a Table That Feels Like Home
URL: https://dena-restaurant.com/blog/den-haag-food-guide-fresh-cozy-restaurants.html

For visitors to Den Haag looking for fresh food, atmosphere, and a genuinely special meal. Den Haag is a food city — internationally diverse, with owner-run restaurants where someone is actually cooking. Prinsestraat is one of the streets worth walking.

The Oven Grilled Fish (€37.50) is DENA's most surprising dish: daily-fresh North Sea fish combined with Persian herb butter and saffron. The fish changes with what is fresh. The stews — Fesenjoon, Ghormeh Sabzi, Zereshk Polo — are cooked for hours. None of these dishes exist anywhere else in Den Haag.

The room is small and warm. Persian culture places enormous value on hospitality to guests. You are not a customer here so much as someone who has been invited to eat.
